content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Part 1 of “How It Actually Works” a series breaking down the systems every backend engineer depends on but few can explain end to end.

You type example.com and hit enter. The page loads in under a second. It feels like one action.
It isn’t. Before a single byte of HTML moves, your machine has to answer a question it cannot answer on its own: what IP address is this name? Computers route packets to numbers. Humans remember names. DNS is the translation layer between the two, and it’s one of the few systems in computing that every single request depends on and almost nobody thinks about until it breaks.
Here’s what actually happens in that gap.
The problem DNS solves
There is no central file listing every domain on the internet. There can’t be — there are hundreds of millions of them, changing constantly, owned by different organizations across every jurisdiction on earth.
So DNS distributes the answer. Instead of one server that knows everything, it’s a tree where each level knows only enough to point you one step closer. The root knows who runs .com. The .com servers know who runs example.com. And the server that runs example.com knows its actual IP address.
No single machine holds the whole map. Each one holds a referral.
The nine steps of a lookup
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s the core visual what happens when you type a URL:1. The browser checks itself first. Chrome and Firefox keep their own small DNS cache. If you visited the site a minute ago, the lookup ends here in microseconds.
2. The OS stub resolver takes over. Your operating system has its own cache, and before that it checks the hosts file the reason 127.0.0.1 localhost works without any network at all. This is also why editing /etc/hosts overrides the entire internet for that one domain, a trick worth remembering when you're testing a migration.
3. The query goes to a recursive resolver. This is usually your ISP’s server, or one you chose deliberately Cloudflare’s 1.1.1.1, Google's 8.8.8.8. The word recursive is the important part. Your machine asks one question and gets one answer. The resolver is the component that does all the walking.
4. The resolver asks a root name server. There are 13 root server addresses, operated by 12 different organizations, served globally through anycast so the “13 servers” are really hundreds of physical machines. The root doesn’t know where example.com lives. It replies with something more useful: ask the servers responsible for .com.
5. The resolver asks the TLD name server. The .com servers don't know the IP either. They know which name servers the domain's owner registered. They reply: ask ns1.example-host.com.
6. The resolver asks the authoritative name server. This one actually knows. It holds the zone file the source of truth for that domain and it returns the A record: the IPv4 address. Or the AAAA record, for IPv6.
7. The resolver caches the answer. For however long the record’s TTL says.
8. The answer flows back to your OS, then your browser, each caching it on the way.
9. Only now does the actual connection begin — the TCP handshake, then TLS, then finally the HTTP request. DNS was the prologue.
The part that matters in production: caching and TTL
Here’s the thing about those nine steps most of the time, they don’t happen. The overwhelming majority of DNS queries are answered by a cache within the first three steps. The full walk down the tree is the cold path, not the hot one.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

this is the part most engineers get wrong in interviewsEvery DNS record carries a TTL (time to live), in seconds. It’s a contract with every cache that sees the record: you may hold this answer for this long. And it forces a real trade-off.
A low TTL (say 60 seconds) means changes propagate almost immediately. If your primary server dies and you repoint the record, traffic follows within a minute. The cost is query volume every cache expires constantly and re-asks, which means more load and slightly higher average latency for your users.
A high TTL (say 24 hours) means fewer lookups, lower cost, and faster average response times. The cost is that you lose control. If you change the record, some resolvers somewhere will keep serving the old IP for a full day, and there is nothing you can do to force them to stop.
This is why “DNS propagation” is a misleading phrase. Nothing propagates. Nothing is pushed anywhere. Caches simply expire at different times, and the internet slowly stops remembering the old answer.
The practical rule: before a planned migration, drop the TTL to 60 seconds and wait for the old TTL to fully expire everywhere. Then make the change. Then raise it back. Teams that skip the first step and just flip the record are the ones still fielding “some users see the old site” tickets two days later.
Record types worth knowing

• A — maps a name to an IPv4 address. The one you think of as “DNS.”

• AAAA — same, for IPv6.

• CNAME — an alias pointing one name at another name. Cannot coexist with other records at the same node, which is why you can’t put a CNAME on a root domain like example.com (hence ALIAS and ANAME, vendor-specific workarounds).

• MX — where to deliver mail for this domain.

• NS — which name servers are authoritative. This is the record that makes delegation work at all.

• TXT — arbitrary text. In practice: SPF, DKIM, and domain ownership verification.
Why this shows up in interviews
DNS is a near-perfect system design question because it’s a real, working example of concepts that are otherwise abstract:
It’s hierarchical delegation no node knows everything, each knows who to ask next. It’s caching with invalidation trade-offs the TTL decision is the same decision you make with Redis, with CDN headers, with any cache you’ll ever design. It’s eventual consistency in production, visible to anyone who’s watched a record change roll out unevenly. And it’s high availability through anycast and redundancy 13 root addresses, hundreds of machines, no single point of failure.
When someone asks “what happens when you type a URL into a browser,” DNS is the first ten seconds of the answer, and how you handle it signals whether you actually understand distributed systems or just memorized the steps.
The one-line summary
DNS is a globally distributed, hierarchical, cache-heavy key-value store where the keys are names, the values are addresses, and the TTL is you choosing between agility and efficiency.

Next in this series: SQL vs NoSQL — what “scales better” actually means when you look at the storage engine.
I’m a senior software engineer working across distributed backends, cloud infrastructure, and event-driven systems. I write one of these breakdowns each week. If you’re building something in this space — or hiring for it — I’d like to hear from you.
